articles.title: Brickbat: Bad Memory
articles.content:
官员们放弃了对此案的追诉。

但事实是，乔治亚州梅肯-比布县的官员们在意识到自己20多年前就已批准了保拉·伊斯特更换的乙烯基窗户后，撤回了对她的诉讼。

现年95岁的伊斯特住在一栋1860年的历史排屋里。一位邻居投诉她的窗户风格不真实，损害了社区转售价值。设计审查委员会曾裁定伊斯特无需更换窗户，但县规划委员会在5月投票推翻这一裁决，威胁她如果在60天内不安装合规窗户，将面临罚款甚至监禁。委员会主席声称伊斯特“公然违反”了分区规则，“明知故犯”地安装了这些窗户。

在伊斯特的孙女和其他人提出抗议后，规划人员发现了一份2002年的批准证书，证明县里当时已批准了现有窗户。意识到这一错误后，委员们撤销了此案。
articles.summary:
Officials in Macon–Bibb County, Georgia, have dropped their case against 95-year-old Paula East after realizing they had approved her vinyl windows more than 20 years ago. East lives in a historic row house from 1860, and a neighbor complained that her windows were inauthentic to the style and hurt neighborhood resale value. A design review board had sided with East, saying she didn't have to replace her windows, but the county zoning commission voted in May to overrule that decision, threatening East with fines or jail time if she didn't install compliant windows within 60 days. The commission chair claimed East "defied" the zoning rules and "knew what she was doing" by installing the windows. After East's granddaughter and others fought the case, planners found a certificate showing they had approved the current windows in 2002. After realizing their mistake, the commissioners closed the case.

The post Brickbat: Bad Memory appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: The Supreme Court Reverses Inferior Court Supremacy In Alabama
articles.content:
周二深夜，最高法院批准了阿拉巴马州的紧急请求，根据Callais案修订其立法地图。附带意见对Callais案进行了便捷总结，并明确将该原则扩展至选票稀释领域。案情分析仅占一段，但我关注的重点是其中一句关键论述：

“关于故意选票稀释，地区法院没有遵循立法善意推定，参见Alexander v. South Carolina State Conference of the NAACP, 602 U.S. 1, 10 (2024)，因为它将州政府对法院先前补救令的法律异议解读为歧视性意图的证据。参见Abbott v. Perez, 585 U.S. 579, 608–609 (2018)。”

阅读阿拉巴马州地区法院的命令时，我有同样的反应：不遵从已被撤销的法院命令不能被用来证明歧视意图。最高法院撤销地区法院命令的决定使该命令归于无效，仿佛它从未存在过。地区法院随后不能声称未能遵从其不存在的命令是意图的证据。我本打算就此撰写文章，但未能如愿。（感谢Betsy法官。）现在正是时候。

我曾撰文讨论下级法院至上主义，即单一地区法院可以确定宪法含义的理念。CASA v. Trump在很大程度上驳斥了这种假设，明确确立了最高法院司法至上原则。我对CASA的这一方面有异议，但那已是最高法律。现在，最高法院在Allen v. Milligan案紧急程序中的命令进一步阐明了下级法院是多么“不够至上”。

诉讼当事人，包括州政府，不应违反具有约束力的法院命令。这样做会导致藐视法庭，但此处并未发生这种情况。地区法院命令阿拉巴马州采用新地图。阿拉巴马州选择采用该州认为符合最高法院先例的地图。地区法院不同意。州有权采用该新地图，知道它会在法院受到挑战，最高法院将根据强制管辖权管辖权最终解决此事。这或多或少是阿利托大法官在Abbott v. Perez一案引述页面中讨论的内容。

这里不存在对任何具有约束力的法院命令的违抗。然而，索托马约尔大法官的异议反复指控该州违抗和藐视地区法院的命令：

“阿拉巴马州以毫不掩饰的公然违抗先前直接经本院确认的法院命令的方式通过了新地图。”

“这也腐蚀了法治，因为它奖励了阿拉巴马州的策略游戏和对法院命令的公然违抗。”

“其次，法院本不应奖励阿拉巴马州在整个诉讼过程中对法院命令的违抗和公然的策略游戏。”

“然而，阿拉巴马州的手远非干净。相反，它违抗了地区法院在这些案件中的命令，即使在本院确认之后……。”

“首先，阿拉巴马州故意选择藐视本院在Allen案中确认的临时禁令。”

索托马约尔大法官解释说，阿拉巴马州希望最高法院会在上诉中同意该州的立场：

当然，阿拉巴马州有权通过对上诉程序或其他方式提出其与地区法院最初临时禁令的“法律异议”，ante, at 3。但该州在此选择的行动方案并非正确的做法。如果阿拉巴马州遵从了临时禁令并绘制了一幅包含第二个机会选区的地图，它可以在普通程序中重新审理案件实质：首先在案件实质审判中，然后在上诉中。在任一阶段，它都可以提出Callais案诉讼当事人提出并胜诉的论点，或对地区法院的补救令提出挑战。相反，阿拉巴马州故意绘制了一幅藐视地区法院临时禁令的地图，并希望本院最终会以该州的方式看待问题。今天之后，很难将阿拉巴马州的愤世嫉俗的赌博称为成功，法院对阿拉巴马州行为的奖励也很难不称为对法治的打击。

索托马约尔大法官还引用了阿拉巴马州众议院议长的话：

记录中没有任何证据表明阿拉巴马州认真对待了本院在Allen案中关于歧视性选票稀释的认定。阿拉巴马州众议院议长纳撒尼尔·莱德贝特直言不讳地说：“'如果你想想我们所处的位置，最高法院在Allen案中的裁决是五比四。所以只需要一位法官改变立场就能改变结果。'”

这种行为只有在您接受地区法院可以裁决这些宪法案件的概念时才算不当。但如果您同意CASA案的观点——最高法院至上，那么阿拉巴马州的行动是检验新地图有效性的唯一途径。诉讼当事人完全有权希望某一阶段以5比4通过的判决在另一阶段也能以5比4通过。多布斯案之所以到达最高法院，是因为密西西比州想要检验Roe案。Callais案之所以到达最高法院，是因为路易斯安那州想要检验Gingles案。Allen案也因类似原因多次往返于最高法院。索托马约尔大法官在这一点上是错误的。

本文《最高法院在阿拉巴马州撤销下级法院至上主义》首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
Late Tuesday evening, the Supreme Court granted Alabama's emergency motion to revise its legislative maps following Callais. The per curiam opinion offers a handy summary of Callais and expressly extends that doctrine to the vote dilution context. The merits analysis stretches one paragraph, but here I want to focus on a single critical sentence:

As to intentional vote dilution, the District Court did not heed the presumption of legislative good faith, see Alexander v. South Carolina State Conference of the NAACP, 602 U. S. 1, 10 (2024), because it interpreted the State's legal disagreement with the court's earlier remedial order as proof of discriminatory animus. Cf. Abbott v. Perez, 585 U. S. 579, 608–609 (2018).

When I read the Alabama district court's order, I had the same reaction: not following a court's order that was vacated cannot be used to demonstrate a discriminatory intent. The Supreme Court's decision to vacate the district court's order rendered that order null and void, as if it had never existed. The district court cannot then turn around and claim that the failure to follow its non-existent order is proof of animus. I meant to write on this point, but I didn't get a chance. (Thanks Judge Betsy.) Now is a good time.

I've written about inferior court supremacy, the notion that a single district court can settle the meaning of the Constitution. CASA v. Trump went a long way to rebutting this presumption by firmly establishing the principle of Supreme Court judicial supremacy. I have problems with this aspect of CASA, but that is now the supreme law of the land. Now, the Supreme Court's emergency docket order in Allen v. Milligan further clarifies how unsuperior the inferior courts are.

Litigants, including state governments, should not violate a binding court order. Doing so would lead to contempt of court, which did not happen here. Instead, the district court ordered Alabama to adopt a new map. Alabama chose to adopt a map that the state found consistent with Supreme Court precedent. The District Court disagreed. The state had the right to adopt that new map, knowing it would be challenged in court, and the Supreme Court would ultimately resolve the matter on its mandatory jurisdiction docket. This is more-or-less what Justice Alito discussed on the cited pages of Abbott v. Perez.

There was no defiance of any binding court order. However, Justice Sotomayor's dissent, repeatedly charges the state with defying and flouting the district court's order:

"Alabama adopted in unashamed defiance of a prior court order directly affirmed by this Court"

"It also corrodes the rule of law by rewarding Alabama's gamesmanship and outright defiance of court orders."

"Second, the Court should not have rewarded Alabama's defiance of court orders and blatant gamesmanship throughout this litigation."

"Alabama's hands, however, are far from clean. Instead, it defied the District Court's order in these cases even after this Court affirmed it. . . ."

"First, Alabama intentionally chose to flout a preliminary injunction that this Court affirmed in Allen."

Justice Sotomayor explains that Alabama hoped the Supreme Court would agree with the state on appeal:

Of course, Alabama had every right to raise its "legal disagreement," ante, at 3, with the District Court's original preliminary injunction through the appellate process orotherwise. The course of action the State chose here, however, was not the proper way of doing so. Had Alabama complied with the preliminary injunction and drawn a map with a second opportunity district, it could have relitigated the merits in the ordinary course: first at a trial on the merits, and then on appeal. At either stage, it could have raised the arguments that the litigants in Callais raised and prevailed on, or advanced challenges to the District Court's remedial order. Instead, Alabama willfully drew a map that flouted the District Court's preliminary injunction and hoped that this Court would eventually see things its way. After today, it is hard to call Alabama's cynical gambit anything other than a success, and the Court's rewarding of Alabama's behavior anything other than a blow to the rule of law.

Sotomayor also quotes from the Speaker of the Alabama House:

The record is bereft of evidence suggesting that Alabama took seriously this Court's finding of discriminatory vote dilution in Allen. Speaker of the Alabama House of Representatives Nathaniel Ledbetter put it bluntly: "'If you think about where we were, the Supreme Court ruling [in Allen] was five to four. So there's just one judge that needed to see something different.'"

This action is only improper if you accept the notion that district courts can settle these constitutional cases. But if you agree with CASA that the Supreme Court is supreme, then Alabama's actions are the only way to test the validity of a new maps. And litigants are well within their rights to hope a 5-4 decision at one stage of the litigation goes 5-4 the other way. Dobbs got to the Supreme Court because Mississippi wanted to test Roe. Callais got to the Supreme Court because Louisiana wanted to test Gingles. And Allen made several trips to the Supreme Court for similar reasons. Justice Sotomayor is wrong on this point.

The post The Supreme Court Reverses Inferior Court Supremacy In Alabama appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Trump's Self-Promotion Is Always Shameless and Sometimes Illegal
articles.content:
特朗普总统长期以来有以自己名字命名事物的历史，包括特朗普大厦、特朗普国家高尔夫俱乐部、特朗普泰姬陵赌场、特朗普大学、特朗普牛排、特朗普伏特加和《特朗普：游戏》。但正如他上周发现的，当这种自我推销需要国会批准时，可能会在法律上遇到麻烦。

上周五，联邦法官裁定，特朗普任命的官员在将肯尼迪表演艺术中心冠以他的名字时超越了法定权限。这一裁决再次提醒人们，这位总统在急于美化自己时往往会践踏法治。

肯尼迪中心理事会由特朗普本人担任主席，成员多为他的亲信，该理事会于12月18日批准了更名决议，更名立即反映在建筑正面的标识上。新名称也出现在该中心的网站、标志和电子邮件中。

美国地区法官克里斯托弗·库珀驳回了这一做法：国会已明确确立了肯尼迪中心的名称，只有国会才能更改。特朗普不出所料地对这一侵犯其虚荣心的裁决感到恼火，称“库珀法官应该为自己感到羞耻！”

暗示特朗普熟悉羞耻概念，似乎与该案中的行为不符。这绝非特朗普利用职权满足其对公众崇拜渴望的唯一一次争议。

在特朗普将名字加在肯尼迪中心之前几周，国务院宣布，国会于1984年建立的美国和平研究所已被重新命名，以纪念“我国历史上最伟大的交易撮合者”。这一变更同样反映在建筑正面，同样难以与联邦法律相协调。

特朗普还将自己的名字用于一类新战舰。但与肯尼迪中心和和平研究所不同，这些舰船尚未建造，可能永远得不到国会资金。

“特朗普金卡”似乎更加可疑。这个项目是特朗普于9月声称授权的，拟通过向美国财政部“捐款”100万美元来吸引外国投资者并给予其永久居留权。但由于国会未批准此项目，其法律依据需要重新定义EB-1和EB-2签证的法定标准，而这些标准取决于财富以外的资质。

特朗普政府仍创建了一个网站，为支付15000美元“处理费”的富有潜在移民提供排队位置。考虑到该项目可疑的合法性，这个承诺似乎与特朗普去年秋天在椭圆形办公室展示的夸张金卡模型一样虚假——上面有自由女神像、一只秃鹰、特朗普的头像和他的签名。

特朗普的面孔和签名也将成为国务院计划发行的“纪念美国护照”的核心元素，据称是为了纪念国家250岁生日。特朗普的签名也会出现在美国货币上，占据通常留给美国财政部长的一席之地——财政部长斯科特·贝森特称这是“认可我们伟大国家和唐纳德·特朗普总统历史性成就的有力方式”。

这两项致敬均属史无前例，但尚属合法。而创造印有特朗普肖像的250美元钞票的大胆计划则不同——它需要国会议员乔·威尔逊去年提出的法案才能实现，但该法案未能通过。这凸显了一个特朗普已经理解的现实：通过合法途径做事很困难。
articles.summary:
President Donald Trump has a long history of naming things after himself, including Trump Tower, the Trump National Golf Club, the Trump Taj Mahal casino, Trump University, Trump Steaks, Trump Vodka, and Trump: The Game. But as he discovered last week, such self-promotion can be legally problematic when it requires congressional approval.

On Friday, a federal judge ruled that Trump's appointees exceeded their statutory authority when they attached his name to the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts. The decision was the latest reminder of the president's tendency to trample the rule of law in his rush to glorify himself.

The Kennedy Center's Board of Trustees, which is chaired by Trump himself and stacked with his cronies, approved the name change on December 18, and it was immediately reflected in the lettering on the front of the building. The new name was also featured in the center's website, logo, and emails.

Not so fast, said U.S. District Judge Christopher Cooper: Congress had clearly established the Kennedy Center's name, and only Congress could change it. Trump was predictably perturbed by that assault on his vanity, saying, "Judge Cooper should be ashamed of himself!"

The implication that Trump is familiar with the concept of shame seemed inconsistent with the conduct at issue in that case. And this was by no means the only time that Trump has courted controversy by using his position to quench his thirst for public adulation.

A couple of weeks before Trump slapped his name on the Kennedy Center, the State Department announced that the U.S. Institute of Peace, a think tank that Congress established in 1984, had been renamed to honor "the greatest dealmaker in our nation's history." That change, which likewise was reflected on the front of the building, was similarly hard to reconcile with federal law.

Trump also has lent his name to a new class of battleships. But unlike the Kennedy Center and the U.S. Institute of Peace, those vessels have not been built yet and may never be funded by Congress.

The "Trump Gold Card" seems even iffier. That program, which Trump purported to authorize in September, is supposed to lure foreign investors by giving them permanent resident status in exchange for a "contribution" of $1 million to the U.S. Treasury. But because Congress has not approved any such program, the legal rationale for it requires rewriting the statutory criteria for EB-1 and EB-2 visas, which hinge on qualifications distinct from sheer wealth.

The Trump administration nevertheless created a website that offers a place in line to wealthy would-be immigrants who fork over a $15,000 "processing fee." But given the program's dubious legality, that promise seems just as phony as the outsized mockup of the Trump Gold Card that the president displayed in the Oval Office last fall, which featured the Statue of Liberty, a bald eagle, a head shot of Trump, and his signature.

Trump's face and signature are also central elements of the "commemorative U.S. passports" that the State Department plans to start issuing soon, ostensibly in honor of the nation's 250th birthday. Trump's signature, but not his face, will appear on American currency too, occupying the spot usually reserved for the U.S. treasurer—a revision that Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent describes as a "powerful way to recognize the historic achievements of our great country and President Donald J. Trump."

Both of those tributes are unprecedented but legal. The same cannot be said of the fanciful plan to create a $250 bill featuring Trump's picture, which would violate federal law in two ways: by honoring a living person and by creating a new denomination.

Last year, Rep. Joe Wilson (R–S.C.) introduced a bill that would have eliminated those obstacles and required the Treasury Department to "commemorate the semiquincentennial of the United States" by producing the Trump bills. But that legislation went nowhere, underlining a point that Trump already understood: Doing things the legal way is hard.

© Copyright 2026 by Creators Syndicate Inc.

The post Trump's Self-Promotion Is Always Shameless and Sometimes Illegal appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Bernie Sanders' AI Wealth Fund Bill Shows That He Doesn't Understand AI or Wealth
articles.content:
周一，联邦参议员伯尼·桑德斯（来自佛蒙特州的独立人士）在《纽约时报》发表评论文章，宣布将在“未来几周内”提出《美国人工智能主权财富基金法案》。桑德斯的法案将通过“对公司股票一次性征收50%税”来创建“主权财富基金”，赋予美国人在该国最大人工智能公司中的“直接所有权份额”。

尽管法案具体细节尚未公布，桑德斯表示，该法案将“让公众在决定人工智能未来方面发挥直接作用”，而不是让使用人工智能的权力“由少数大型科技寡头决定”。桑德斯的提案还将允许联邦政府使用其“投票股份和每个公司董事会的同等代表权”来阻止其认为有害的决策和政策。

桑德斯的计划建立在学术界以及OpenAI、Anthropic和xAI（美国三家最大的人工智能公司）领导人的类似呼吁基础上，这些人士主张建立一种为美国人提供直接付款的正式程序。去年2月，唐纳德·特朗普总统发布了一项行政命令，指示财政部长和商务部长以及总统经济政策助理“在90天内制定一项计划”，提交给总统。

该基金的细节尚未公布。财政部和商务部未回应《理性》杂志的置评请求。

为了支持自己的论点，桑德斯引用了挪威政府全球养老基金和阿拉斯加永久基金作为值得借鉴的范例，尽管这两个基金都依靠石油和天然气钻探的收入设立，而桑德斯强烈反对这些活动。

桑德斯关于财富基金应该提供“直接支付给美国人民”的计划完全基于阿拉斯加永久基金的范例。1976年，阿拉斯加通过了一项宪法修正案和一系列后续法律，保证在该州居住至少12个月的任何人都能从该州的财富基金获得分红。如今，这个860亿美元基金的年度分红每年波动，因为政府从中抽取资金以充实国库。然而，据Bankrate数据显示，与典型的低成本指数基金相比，阿拉斯加的财富基金表现相当不错，但管理费用要高得多。

与此同时，挪威的基金限制其立法者每年只能支出该基金的3%，而该国在保持投资“去政治化”方面一直面临困难，因为政治家和公众在哪些倡议和公司足够合乎道德值得资助的问题上争论不休。

虽然桑德斯将“科技寡头”描绘成现代版的强盗贵族，但他提出的这个想法实际上是世界各地真正的寡头和威权主义者常用的手段，用来支撑不自由的政权、非法输送资金，并对其公民行使不受约束的权力。

在俄罗斯，弗拉基米尔·普京总统正在掏空该国的国家财富基金用于对乌克兰的战争，这违背了该国金融监管机构的建议。伊朗利用其国家发展基金为真主党、哈马斯及其影子警察部队等恐怖组织提供资金，而据人权观察组织2024年的一份报告称，沙特阿拉伯的财富基金经常被用来实施人权侵犯。虽然美国的财富基金不太可能被如此恶意地使用，但最近的欺诈案件表明，假设一笔数百亿美元不受监管的资金可能会诱惑官员并非不合理。

桑德斯似乎还根本误解了人工智能正在造福大多数美国人，而不仅仅是超级富豪。富达投资的一份退休报告显示，截至2026年第一季度，401(k)账户的平均余额比前一年增长了11%。

人工智能也在创造非物质收益。人工智能检测工具可以更早、更准确地识别乳腺癌，而双语对话代理已被证明能够在早期改善学生的语言和词汇能力。如果人工智能的每一项进步都要经过政府批准，正如桑德斯所提议的那样，像这样的突破不太可能以社会所要求的速度和规模实现。

值得庆幸的是，该法案不太可能通过。尽管如此，桑德斯提出这一想法的随意态度表明，来自两党的更多立法者希望监管并放缓一项技术——正如桑德斯所写，这可能是“世界历史上最具变革性的技术”。

这篇《伯尼·桑德斯的人工智能财富基金法案表明他既不了解人工智能也不了解财富》文章首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
On Monday, in a New York Times op-ed, Sen. Bernie Sanders (I–Vt.) announced plans to introduce the American A.I. Sovereign Wealth Fund Act "in the coming weeks." Sanders' bill would give Americans a "direct ownership stake" in the country's largest AI companies by creating "a sovereign wealth fund through a one-time 50 percent tax" of company stock.

While specifics of the legislation haven't been shared, Sanders says the bill will "give the public a direct role in determining the future" of AI, rather than its use being "dictated by a handful of Big Tech oligarchs." Sanders' proposal would also allow the federal government to use its "voting shares and an equal representation on each company's board" to block decisions and policies it deems harmful.

Sanders' plan builds on similar calls to action from academics and the leaders of OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI—three of the country's largest AI companies—advocating for a formalized process that provides Americans direct payments from the industry. President Donald Trump issued an executive order last February directing the secretaries of the Treasury Department and the Commerce Department, as well as the assistant to the president for economic policy, to "develop a plan" for a sovereign wealth fund and submit it to the president within 90 days.

Details of this fund have yet to be released. The Treasury Department and the Commerce Department did not respond to Reason's request for comment.

To bolster his argument, Sanders cites Norway's Government Pension Fund Global and the Alaska Permanent Fund Corporation as examples worth following, though both of them are funded by revenue from oil and gas drilling, which Sanders vehemently opposes.

Sanders' plan that a wealth fund should provide "direct payments to the American people" rests solely on the example of Alaska's permanent fund. In 1976, Alaska passed a constitutional amendment and a subsequent series of laws guaranteeing a payout from the state's wealth fund to anyone who resides in the state for at least 12 months. Today, payouts from the $86 billion fund fluctuate from year to year as the government siphons money from it to bolster its coffers. Still, compared to a typical low-cost index fund, Alaska's wealth fund performs admirably, but with much higher management fees, according to Bankrate data.

Norway's fund, meanwhile, restricts its lawmakers from spending more than 3 percent of the fund annually, and the country has struggled to remain "apolitical" in its investments, as politicians and the public haggle over which initiatives and companies are ethical enough to fund.

And while Sanders frames "tech oligarchs" as modern-day robber barons, he proposes an idea commonly used by real oligarchs and authoritarians across the world to prop up illiberal regimes, illegally funnel money, and wield unchecked power over their citizens.

In Russia, President Vladimir Putin is draining the country's National Wealth Fund for his war in Ukraine, against the advice of the nation's financial monitors. Iran uses its National Development Fund to finance terrorist groups such as Hezbollah, Hamas, and its shadow police force, while Saudi Arabia's wealth fund is regularly used to facilitate human rights abuses, according to a 2024 report from Human Rights Watch. While it's unlikely that an American wealth fund would be used this nefariously, recent cases of fraud show it's not unreasonable to assume that an unappropriated pot of hundreds of billions of dollars could tempt officials.

Sanders also appears to fundamentally misunderstand that AI is benefiting most Americans, not just the ultrarich. A retirement report from Fidelity Investments found that through the first quarter of 2026, the average 401(k) account balance was up 11 percent from the previous year.

It's also creating nonmaterial gains. AI detection tools can identify breast cancer earlier and more accurately, while bilingual conversational agents have been shown to improve students' language and vocabulary at an early age. If every advancement in AI is subject to government approval, as Sanders proposes, it's unlikely that breakthroughs like these would be achieved at the pace and scale society demands.

Thankfully, it's unlikely that the bill will pass. Still, Sanders' comfort in proposing the idea indicates that more lawmakers from both sides of the aisle want to regulate and slow down a technology that, as Sanders writes, could be "the most transformational technology in the history of the world."

The post Bernie Sanders' AI Wealth Fund Bill Shows That He Doesn't Understand AI or Wealth appeared first on Reason.com.
